content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 6 – “P” for ProductPart 6 – Managing New Product Development, Product Life Cycle and Additional Consideration
Chapter 8 - New products: Developing and managing innovation
from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing, 8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Part 6 - Managing new-product development, - Product life-cycle strategies and - Additional product and service considerations

Managing new-product development (1 of 3)
Customer-centred new-product development
Customer-centred new-product development focuses on finding new ways to solve customers’ problems and create more customer-satisfying experiences.
Studies show that the most successful new products:
are differentiated
solve major customer problems
offer a compelling customer value proposition
directly engaged customers in the new-product innovation process.

Managing new-product development (2 of 3)
Team-based new-product development
Sequential product development is a new-product development approach in which one company department works individually to complete its stage of the process before passing the new product along to the next department.
Team-based new-product development is an approach in which company departments work together in cross-functional teams, overlapping the steps in the product-development process to both save time and increase effectiveness.
Q: What are some advantages and disadvantages of each of these approaches?

Managing new-product development
Q: What are some advantages and disadvantages of each of these approaches?
Sequential product development - one department completes its stage before passing along to the next department.
Pros: Clear delineation of responsibility, each stage is complete before passing-on.
Cons: Slower. In the next stage a problem might crop-up which requires changes to be made to work done in a previous stage. Much expenditure made in earlier stages before (1) Business case found to be negative, or (2) product might be found impracticable to make or offer at large scale.
Team-based new-product development departments work together in cross-functional teams, overlapping the steps.
Pros: save time and increase effectiveness.
Cons: multiple problems happening at the same time make it difficult to identify the cause/s. Might be difficult to clearly assign responsibility.

Managing new-product development (3 of 3)
Systematic new-product development
The new-product development process should be holistic and systematic, rather than compartmentalised and haphazard.
A company can install an innovation management system to collect, review, evaluate and manage new-product ideas.
An innovation management system enables:
an innovation-oriented company culture
a larger selection of new-product ideas
more systematic development of new-product ideas.

Product life-cycle strategies (1 of 7)
The product life cycle is the course of a product’s sales and profits during its lifetime. It involves five distinct stages:
product development – a period of slow sales, no profits and high investment costs
introduction – a period of slow sales growth but with non-existent profits
growth – a period of market acceptance and increasing profits
maturity – a period of slowdown in sales growth
decline – the period when both sales and profits drop.
Q: Name some products in each of these stages: growth, maturity and decline.

Figure 8.2
Sales and profits over the product life cycle, from inception to decline

Product life-cycle strategies (3 of 7)
The product life-cycle (PLC) concept can also be applied to each of the following:
style – a basic and distinctive mode of expression
fashion – a currently accepted or popular style in a given field
fad – a fashion that enters quickly, is adopted with great zeal, peaks early and declines quickly.

Figure 8.3
Styles, fashions and fads
Q: What are some examples of each of a style, fashion and fad?

Q: Name some products in each of these stages: growth, maturity and decline.
A: Growth: electric vehicles, Maturity: streaming services, Decline: Free-to-Air television.
Product life-cycle strategies
Q: What are some examples of each of a style, fashion and fad?
A: Style: “old-money” and “classic” clothing styles, Fashion: baggy jeans and tops, Fad: Yo-Yos toy),

Additional product and service considerations (1 of 3)
Product decisions and social responsibility
Marketers should carefully consider public policy issues and regulations regarding:
acquiring new products
dropping products
patent protection
product quality and safety
product warranties
product risk and liability.
Q: Outline some public policy issues and/or regulations in regard to two of the product decisions listed above.

Additional product and service considerations (2 of 3)
International product and services marketing
Companies must decide:
what products and services to introduce and into which countries
how much to standardise or adapt their products and services for world markets.
Standardisation:
helps a company develop a consistent worldwide image
also lowers the product design, manufacturing and marketing costs of offering a large variety of products.
Packaging presents new challenges for international marketers.

Additional product and service considerations (3 of 3)
International product and services marketing (cont.)
Services marketers that have gone global include:
commercial banking
professional and business services (e.g. accounting, management consulting, advertising)
retailers.
Q: Give some examples of service companies operating internationally. What special challenges do such companies face?

Additional product and service considerations
Q: Outline some public policy issues and/or regulations in regard to two of the product decisions listed below.
acquiring new products – check that there are no overhanging potential liabilities, such as damage claims regarding product safety or waste disposal issues
dropping products – some users may be dependent upon the product and dropping it might not be permitted (medical products). Redundant employees will need to be compensated. Customers need to be given fair notice of the discontinuance. Service and spare parts will need to be made available for a certain period. Disposing of the production facilities and remaining stock and materials might involve expensive environmental clean-ups.
patent protection – if we have a patent, when does it run out? Are competitors violating it? Has it been registered overseas? Are we marketing the product in such a way as to maintain the patent? If we do not have a patent, can we/should we get one? In which markets? Is it worth the cost?

Additional product and service considerations
product quality and safety – ensure current products meet all safety requirements and that our products meet our own quality standards and customer expectations. Ensure all products come with safety warnings and user-instructions regarding correct-use and safety. See also below >>
product warranties – what commitments have been made, what will be the likely costs of honouring the warranties, have financial provisions be made, and are they sufficient? Do we have the service staff/spare parts/materials to service and make repairs under warranty?
product risk and liability – what is the risk assessment, and what are our forecasted liabilities? What can we do to limit future product risk by taking actions such as retro-fixing, issuing revised user safety instructions, recalling known-faulty products and repairing or replacing them at no-charge. Have financial provisions be made, and are they sufficient?

Additional product and service considerations
Q: Give some examples of service companies operating internationally. What special challenges do such companies face?
Examples: J Walter Thompson (advertising), Accenture (consulting), Ernst & Young (auditing), Microsoft (computer software).
Challenges:
Laws are different in every country and might also vary between jurisdictions within the same country (such as states and provinces).
Public opinion about particular industries and companies will vary. Needs image management.
In some countries the professional association and/or local competitors might have undue influence over the regulators.
In some companies the regulators or government might not apply the laws at all, or even-handedly or might take bribes. Paying bribes, even in an overseas country, is illegal for US and Australian companies.
Culture varies from country to country, both in the work-place and in the population: will require different management methods, different marketing communications, perhaps changed products.

“P” for Place
